title: Dataset shape confirms PLANO assumptions
detail: 2479 rows × 989 cols (988 features + Label). Classes balanced at 33%/33.5%/33.5%. No nulls. All float64.

title: Exact duplicate rate: 4.64% (115 rows)
detail: Matches PLANO's expectation. De-dup before split is mandatory — else same window appears in train and test.

title: Outlier severity confirms RobustScaler need
detail: Global max |value| = 530,657 vs p99 = 311. Ratio 1704x. StandardScaler std would be blown up by outliers.

title: No subject IDs in aggregated CSV
detail: No columns matching subject/participant/id/user/session. LOSO validation not feasible from this CSV alone. Logged as known limitation.

title: Frequency axis decoded (external research)
detail: freq_XXX / 100 ≈ Hz, because Bird resampled Muse data to 150Hz with 148-sample FFT window. 50Hz notch-filter gap (UK AC line noise). Band mapping: theta=freq_040-080, alpha=freq_080-130, beta=freq_130-300.

title: Right hemisphere dominates top-20 ANOVA features
detail: AF8 (right frontal): 7 features. TP10 (right temporal): 7. TP9 (left temporal): 6. AF7 (left frontal): ZERO. Consistent with Posner & Petersen (1990) right-hemisphere dominance for sustained attention.

title: Discriminative frequencies cluster at 10-13 Hz (alpha boundary)
detail: Top frequency features: freq_101, freq_111, freq_122, freq_132 (all channels). This is the alpha-band boundary — classical Berger (1929) alpha-blocking effect.

title: ANOVA top-10 is really 4 distinct signals, doubled
detail: Each raw_* feature has a near-identical lag1_* counterpart. Effective informative set is smaller than naive count suggests. Matters for interpretation, not for modeling (L2 handles it).

title: Feature families are lenses, not metadata
detail: 988 features decompose into families: freq_ (576 total, spectral lens), statistics (std/min/max/skew/kurt — distribution-shape lens), covM/eigenval (spatial-coordination lens), lag1_ (temporal-change lens). All are predictors.

title: Engagement score: Option B (model-based proxy)
detail: score = 100 * P(concentrating) from LogReg. Reason: frequency-axis units were not documented in the CSV; BATR would require assumptions we couldn't verify inside the time-box. Documented in DESIGN.md §D2.

title: Scaler: RobustScaler + clip to ±10
detail: After user-prompted empirical check: for ~10% of features (covariance matrix entries, std/IQR ratios 30-248x) the scalers differ meaningfully. RobustScaler preserves typical-value separation; clip bounds the residual outliers.

title: Feature families treated as equal in model input, stratified in analysis
detail: No family-aware regularization (Group Lasso) or hand-weighting. With 2479 rows and 988 features, injecting family priors risks bias without generalization gain. Instead: report XGBoost feature importance aggregated by family.

title: Validation protocol: stratified 80/20 holdout + 5-fold CV on train
detail: CV on train for model selection/stability. Holdout test is locked until final eval — single unbiased number per model in the report.

question: Is RobustScaler really different from StandardScaler when median≈0 and IQR is small?
resolution: Measured empirically. Half of features: std/IQR ≈ 1 (scalers ~identical). For 10% of features (covariance matrix entries): std/IQR is 30-248x — scalers diverge massively. Example: covM_1_1 raw=369k → SS=25.3, RS=6290. Switched to RobustScaler + post-clip to ±10 for bounded inputs.

question: Should we use BATR (Pope 1995) or a model-based engagement score?
resolution: BATR requires knowing which FFT columns map to alpha/beta/theta. The CSV's column names (freq_XXX) didn't label units. Used Option B (model-based proxy). Decoded the axis mapping post-hoc for future work.

question: Are we treating all 988 features as equal? Is that correct given their semantic families?
resolution: In input: yes (uniform L2, no family priors). Correct because (a) no justified family-level prior, (b) L2 + XGBoost handle intra-family redundancy gracefully, (c) hand-crafted priors risk bias with small sample. In analysis: no — will report importance by family in T12.

question: Are lag1_* and non-frequency features just metadata, not real predictors?
resolution: No — every column is a predictor. lag1_X = same statistic on the previous window. std/min/max/covM are legitimate statistical lenses on the signal. ANOVA happens to favor freq_* because they carry cleaner univariate linear separation, but multivariate models use the rest too.

title: Split pipeline verified
detail: 115 duplicates removed, then 80/20 stratified split into 1891/473. Class proportions match across splits (tolerance 1%).

title: Scaler pipeline verified
detail: RobustScaler fit on 1891 train rows, applied with clip ±10.0. Roundtrip (save→load→reapply) matches. Clipped fraction: train=0.942%, test=0.988%.

title: Baseline LogReg established
detail: L2 multinomial, 5-fold CV on 1891 train rows: macro-F1 = 0.9514 ± 0.0075. Folds stable (max-min gap < 2*std). Margin over chance (0.333): +0.6184.

title: XGBoost trained with default hyperparameters
detail: 5-fold CV on 1891 train rows: macro-F1 = 0.9701 ± 0.0085.

title: LogReg→XGB gap = +1.87 pts
detail: LogReg 0.9514 vs XGB 0.9701 CV macro-F1. Small-positive gap → most signal is capturable linearly. Bird's pre-extracted features (FFT, covariance, kurtosis) already do most of the non-linear transformation, so XGBoost only adds marginal interactions. Both models are valid; gap is informative about the problem structure, not about which to ship.

title: Dual model usage: LogReg for scoring, XGB for final metric
detail: LogReg probabilities are calibrated and interpretable — used for engagement score P(concentrating). XGB has higher CV, used as the headline accuracy number for the test set and for the family-level importance analysis. Both saved to artifacts/. No single "winning" model — each plays a role.

question: Are 95%+ CV scores suspiciously high for consumer-grade EEG?
resolution: No, consistent with published benchmarks on this dataset. BUT: only 2 subjects, and CV splits windows randomly. Adjacent windows within a session are near-identical → model partly memorizes session signatures. Real deployment on new users would score lower. This is the session-leakage effect; must be flagged as #1 limitation in REPORT.

title: Held-out test set — final honest numbers
detail: LogReg macro-F1 = 0.9528, XGBoost macro-F1 = 0.9704. Gap on test = +0.0176 (CV gap was +0.0187 — consistent, no suspicious train/test discrepancy). 473 rows, stratified split, scaler and models never saw this data.

title: XGBoost importance dominated by frequency-domain features
detail: Top family: 'freq' with 0.459 summed importance. Ranking: {'freq': 0.4594487249851227, 'eigenval': 0.11125167459249496, 'topFreq': 0.07597670704126358, 'min': 0.06751847267150879, 'max': 0.06310917437076569}. Consistent with ANOVA ranking — both univariate and multivariate views agree that FFT bins carry the most signal.

title: Correction on subject count
detail: Earlier I assumed 2 subjects based on an unverified guess. User correction: the dataset has 4 subjects. This weakens (slightly) the session-leakage concern — more subject diversity means less memorization risk per-session — but does NOT eliminate it. Random window split still bundles adjacent windows from the same subject/session, which are near-identical. The limitation framing stands: CV scores are valid for "new windows from the same 4 subjects", not "new subjects entirely".

question: How many subjects are in the Kaggle CSV?
resolution: User confirmed: 4 subjects, not 2 (my initial unverified assumption). Updated report framing accordingly. Caveat about subject-aware validation still holds — 4 is still a small sample, and LOSO remains the right protocol for a production claim.

title: Dataset demographics (from Kaggle)
detail: 4 participants: 2 men, 2 women. Small and gender-balanced. Still too few for confident subject-independent claims — proper LOSO deployment validation would require far more subjects.

title: Engagement score verified as coherent
detail: Monotonicity OK: relaxed=3.0 < neutral=46.5 < concentrating=99.5. Range [0,100]. Spearman r with ordinal label = 0.9269. Pearson r with binary concentrating indicator = 0.8543. Score shows within-class variance (not collapsed to 3 points), so it's usable as a continuous signal, not just a 3-level proxy.

name: logreg_cv_pipeline_mean
note: honest CV, scaler refit per fold

name: xgb_cv_pipeline_mean
note: honest CV, scaler refit per fold

title: Honest pipeline-CV numbers
detail: LogReg macro-F1 = 0.9534 ± 0.0068. XGBoost macro-F1 = 0.9690 ± 0.0070. Scaler refit per fold inside sklearn Pipeline. Test-set numbers (scripts/evaluate.py) are unaffected — they come from a locked holdout.

title: Held-out test set — final honest numbers
detail: LogReg macro-F1 = 0.9528, XGBoost macro-F1 = 0.9704. Gap on test = +0.0176 (CV gap was +0.0187 — consistent, no suspicious train/test discrepancy). 473 rows, stratified split, scaler and models never saw this data.

title: XGBoost importance dominated by frequency-domain features
detail: Top family: 'freq' with 0.459 summed importance. Ranking: {'freq': 0.4594487249851227, 'eigenval': 0.11125167459249496, 'topFreq': 0.07597670704126358, 'min': 0.06751847267150879, 'max': 0.06310917437076569}. Consistent with ANOVA ranking — both univariate and multivariate views agree that FFT bins carry the most signal.
